html:
Solutions 30 SE: Ugly, Levered, But Not Dead

Solutions 30 is not the slam-dunk cash gusher described in the prior draft. The real 2025 numbers were worse: revenue fell to €892.4m, adjusted EBITDA was €65.2m, net income was -€60.7m, pre-lease free cash flow was only €15.0m, and net free cash flow after leases was -€21.4m. The auditor still flagged material uncertainty around going concern. That matters.

But the stock is now priced as if the transition fails. At about €0.68 per share, the equity value is only about €73m against nearly €900m of revenue and a platform with more than 16,000 technicians. The forward thesis is not “2025 was great.” It is that 2025 was the trough year of a forced refocus away from low-margin French fiber deployment and toward energy, grid modernization, photovoltaic infrastructure, EV charging, Belgian fiber recovery, German connectivity, and recurring maintenance work.

The misunderstood part is the mix. French Connectivity is collapsing and was still 15% of 2025 revenue, but management is deliberately shrinking the least profitable exposure. Excluding French Connectivity, group revenue grew 2.9% in 2025. Energy in France grew 32.3%, Benelux EBITDA margin rose to 12.6%, Germany revenue grew 13.5%, and “Other Countries” EBITDA rose 65.5% with a 230 bp margin improvement. Q1 2026 was still ugly, with revenue down 8.6%, but the message was the same: French Connectivity is being cut, Energy is now the largest contributor in France, Belgian fiber is resuming, and Germany remains structurally attractive.

The market label is “tainted French fiber roll-up with accounting baggage.” The better forward label is “European field-service platform pivoting into grid and energy-transition work while cleaning up a bad telecom cycle.” That label is not yet earned, but it is plausible enough to matter at this valuation.

The asset-light platform has real optionality. Solutions30 acquired control of SO-TEC, a French photovoltaic infrastructure specialist with more than €20m of annual revenue, and bought 51% of Elektra Realizacje in Poland, a low- and medium-voltage grid modernization contractor. These are not huge acquisitions, but they show the direction: away from mature rollout labor and into power-grid, solar, EV, and maintenance work where European capex should remain structurally supported.

The balance sheet is tight, not fatal. At year-end 2025, gross cash was €73.2m, net bank debt was €36.3m, and total net debt including leases and earnouts was €99.6m, or 1.5x EBITDA. There is a €120m seven-year bank facility, with €13.6m unused at year-end. The problem is not a near-term debt wall. The problem is whether the company can convert EBITDA into real cash after leases, working capital, interest, and restructuring.

That is the crux. EBITDA is a poor shortcut here. On 2025 numbers, €65m of adjusted EBITDA turned into only €7m of adjusted EBIT and negative net FCF after leases. The upside case requires margins and working capital to normalize. If 2027 revenue stabilizes around €850m and EBITDA margin recovers to 8.5%, EBITDA becomes roughly €72m. With normalized capex around €11m, lower restructuring cash, lease cash outflows closer to €28m, and modest bank interest, levered free cash flow could be €20m to €25m. That would put today’s stock at roughly 3x normalized levered FCF.

The bull case is not crazy. If France stops bleeding, Benelux keeps double-digit margins, Germany scales, and energy services become a larger share, a 9.5% to 10% EBITDA margin on €900m of revenue gives €85m to €90m of EBITDA. At even 4x to 5x EBITDA, after debt, the equity is worth several times the current market cap. A strategic buyer could underwrite the technician network, customer relationships, and energy-transition footprint far more generously than public markets currently do.

Retail context is mixed and low-quality. The old 2020 to 2021 Reddit and forum chatter was short-squeeze and Muddy Waters drama. Current Boursorama threads are much more skeptical and focused on whether Q2 2026 can confirm a bottom. That is useful because it shows sentiment is still washed out. Nobody is paying a “turnaround already fixed” multiple.

The key catalyst is not a press release. It is the H2 2026 P&L. Management says the French Connectivity restructuring should start helping in the second half of 2026. If H1 remains weak but H2 shows margin stabilization, the stock can rerate before reported net income looks clean. If H2 does not improve, the going-concern warning will stay and the equity deserves to trade like a distressed option.

This is a high-torque, high-risk turnaround. The downside is real because 2025 still burned cash after leases and the auditor still raised going-concern uncertainty. But at €0.68, the market is already capitalizing the whole company at less than one year of plausible normalized free cash flow. That is the setup.

Bear target: €0.25, down 63%. French Connectivity keeps deteriorating, Germany remains choppy, restructuring cash continues, EBITDA falls toward €45m to €50m, and the equity faces dilution or another balance-sheet event.

Base target: €1.35, up 99%. Revenue stabilizes near €850m, EBITDA recovers to roughly €72m to €75m, lease-adjusted FCF reaches €20m to €25m, and the stock gets only a modest 5x to 6x normalized FCF multiple.

Bull target: €3.20, up 371%. Energy, grid, Belgian fiber, Italy, Poland, and Germany offset the French telecom runoff, EBITDA approaches €90m, net debt starts falling again, and the market values Solutions30 at 4x to 5x EBITDA as a cleaned-up infrastructure-services platform rather than a distressed accounting story.
